transcript.text: Anna, how's it going?

Oh, awesome.
Awesome.

Can't be both off mute or it's going to be an echo chamber.
Yeah, we did that.
We did well.
There was zero echo.
Yeah, we're seamless.
Flawless, flawless execution.
What part of the city is Daffelsen?
We're actually really close to you, I think, because you're right across the street from South Park, right?
Exactly.
Yep, that's us.
You're not in the office today, I see.
I actually am based in Houston, so I get to come up like every one to two months.
I'll be there next week.
But yes, it's a nice, you know, I'm not on this island remote every single day.
I get to get some in-person interaction.
I'm looking at where your office is.
Yeah, it's super close to us.
That's really
We're on 3rd Street.
Sorry, you're in Houston.
guess you might not know where that is.
Anyway, it's a five-minute walk away.
Amazing.
Yeah, we host events all the time.
So we'll loop you in the next time we do one of our breakfasts or dinners.
And I lived in SF for three years.
So I know the city well.
I just have to be close to family with my fiance and I starting to move into the next chapter of our lives.
Nice.
That's great.
Very nice.
Cool.
So Tyler, what did Marcel tell you or did he just like chuck you to the little tear?
Yeah, so intro on me, I'm our founding AE and I'm the only person on our sales team.
So before me, it was like all done by Marcel, but he has a lot of other things to do to run a company.
So we worked together quite frequently.
He told me that like you and him had done some early workshops together and...
That the use case for Cognition, it could be very similar to like what we've done for AugmentCode.
So my plan was really just to get more context behind what the partnership could look like and where we could be most helpful.
And then answer any questions that, you know, Anna, you have about GrowthX, but give you like a proper introduction, right, before we just go into Q&A mode.
Yeah.
So by, yeah, so by way of intro, yeah, I've known Marcel.
Marcel's the CEO and founder.
I've known him for years.
And yeah, before he started GrowthX, he did some long post about like massively growing traffic when he was at DeepGram.
I don't know about DeepGram.
It's one of the voice, like model makers.
Yeah.
So, yeah, I've known him at GrowthX.
I used it at a couple companies.
Back when you guys were more closely partnered with Aeroops.
don't know.
again.
for Ogalia, Yeah.
Um,
So we had air ops too with those.
And yeah, I work, Tyler, for your understanding, I'm a consultant.
So I work part-time at Cognition.
One of my areas of concern is growing traffic.
So SEO and content is like the priority for me on this.
I know you guys do a ton of other stuff.
And there's a ton of other stuff that could be interesting.
Anna might ask questions about that, but I'm like, let's not go too deep on it.
I think that's like, let's just think about, let's just think about some classic content and SEO.
And Anna, I'll let you introduce yourself.
Yeah.
So I lead product marketing here at Cognition, but in reality, my role has been really broad working across all different types of marketing.
I was previously at Rippling for three years where I led a lot of our growth initiatives.
So I have a lot of thoughts on what we should be doing on the growth side.
I think, yeah, as Hank mentioned, would really love to dig deep into the organic content and SEO side of the house on this call.
Next, I think on our end is just, you know, Cognition's doing super well as business, but broadly, the market doesn't know enough about us.
I think our competitors, like Cursor, Anthropic, are eating a lot of the mindshare, and we want to improve our organic presence.
We have a lot of really interesting use cases and things to highlight.
We just, like, haven't really had the people in seat, like, to be doing the work.
Like, our team is very, very engineering and sales-heavy, and we're just sort of building out the marketing function for the first time.
So, yeah, I think, like, we have both, like, our business is primarily enterprise-driven, very sales
Sled motion with Devon, which is like an autonomous software engineer and Windsurf, which is an AI IDE.
And we increasingly are selling them kind of as part of the same platform with several other products as well.
We also have a self-serve motion that's kind of earlier days.
But yeah, I would love to explore like content plays that kind of across the whole suite of products.
Yeah, that sounds awesome.
Great to meet you both.
Helpful context.
To your point, Hank, SEO, organic content, that's what we prefer to do, honestly, right?
Like there's a lot of other use cases that we can execute on, posting for social, maybe doing things off-site with Reddit and other platforms.

But the core service that we want to provide as a starting point is helping you create and refresh editorial and programmatic content on your marketing site, where we're publishing like every single business day.
Because that's
That's going to be like the fastest way that we can drive visibility and start to improve your domain authority and increase traffic.
One question I have is just who's creating the content today since the marketing team's pretty lean?
Is it just you, Anna?
Is there other folks involved in the process?
Yeah, it's a mix of folks.
I mean, our engineering team does a lot of like our product blogs and launches.
We also have produced some like much more tactical content, like how to do a .NET to Java migration with Devon, which like our GTM, like Forward Deployed Engineering team will help with.
So it's kind of a mix.
I think we have a lot of people who have a lot of like raw materials for content, but we sort of need like a machine around it.
Like stuff is sitting on people's heads across the team.
That makes sense.
Has anybody tried to like leverage AI to speed up the process to create?
content or even to help with creating content?
Not in a systematic way.
I mean, I think everyone uses AI to speed up their work, but it's not like we've gotten really thoughtful around how to produce use case content at scale.
Yeah, there's no workflow here.
People will prompt stuff to write to give words faster, but there's no workflows.
There's no systems.
That makes sense.
There's not, nor do we have like systems for, or even formality around like brainstorming content, I think.
Everything has been kind of, as far as I can summarize, it's been random acts of content.
Yeah, and like looking at the index pages on SEMrush, that's what I can see as well, right?
Is that there's like a lot of room for opportunity to just like increase the quantity and get some more content out there that could, help you get side in.
AI answers.
To sort of segue into what we do at GrowthX, we don't like to call ourselves an agency because we have this internal platform that powers all of our work.
And next week or the week after, we're launching like our AI visibility and monitoring product called Check That, which our customers would get as part of the relationship.

And so it's really like end to end, identify the right strategy, both with traditional and AI search, develop this roadmap of opportunities that are all concentrated around topic clusters where you want more authority, and then actually execute, even handling the staging and publishing so that your team basically has all the bandwidth to focus on strategic initiatives with content or like with other marketing channels.
So that's the vision at GrowthX high level.
We'll also do all the monitoring visibility so that you don't have to track like where you're showing up and how that's improving.
But Anna, did you have any questions about the scope of the services before I dig deeper?
No, let's jump in.
Let's do it.

So just a quick TLDR, I know Hank knows this, but one reason we feel like we're different is Marcel has always built organic content engines, and it's been a major contributor to the hyper growth that was had at these companies when he was their marketing leader.
And he's done it different ways, right?
Like IBM, they built this microsite called securityintelligence.com.
That was their highest ROI marketing channel for the business.
HP, was a huge contributor network, and they built this newsletter, right?
Like AI Minds was the name of it.
And then at DeepGram, that's where he really thought about, well, how can I use, how can I get ahead of the curve and use workflows to automate or accelerate the process to building these engines?
way, screen.
before equipment?
uneven and
And as Hank mentioned, right, they 24x their traffic in 18 months, they 4x their revenue.
And then he started hosting these workshops while he was CMO at DeepGram.
And nobody wanted to start from zero, you know, building workflows and then refactoring them or maybe having costs scale out of control as you try to make all these API calls to different tools.
And so he started trying to help do some consulting.
And then he basically just built GrowthX, which is the product like he's always wanted as a CMO.

So that's like the origin story behind what we're doing and why a lot of more technical B2B software companies like really trust us to hit the quality bar and also be able to integrate like the context so that the workflows are really personalized.
Who are we writing for?
What's the voice and identity that the brand needs to have?
What are all the most important questions your audience is answering?
your audience is answering?
That's at the intersection of, you how your product's differentiated or the use cases that it unlocks.
Any other questions or any questions now?
Yeah, I think I would love to get a sense of like the range of content types and maybe a few example projects that you think were just like not out of the park.
Amazing.
There are a lot of business impact.
Yeah, absolutely.
So on the content types, this is a good slide that sort of puts everything in one place.
A lot of times we're publishing to the blog, publishing to like a slash guides.
You could think about programmatic examples like for RAMP, we've done this vendor directory, which is one of the fastest growing areas of their site.
The vendor directory, it's basically using their internal spend data to populate each of these pages.

And charts so that, you know, now when folks are looking up Airtable or Airtable comparisons, they start to see RAMP as this authority around business spending, but even business spending with different tools.

So these are like some different use cases that we can execute on and, you we're pretty versatile, like we've even done integrations pages for Webflow by integrating or by ingesting all of their different docs that inform the workflows that we leverage.

And then if you want to think about just a really good editorial example, know, augment code in pretty much the same space, like AI enterprise coding, we've created over 150 guides for them that are, you AI forward, seven tactics for different types of code bases, governance frameworks, tool comparisons as well.
And all of these different pieces of content, a lot of them have code snippets, you know, in them.
And so this is like a good example of how we were able to bring in something more technical for a developer audience.
Do you have any metrics on these?
Like how did this impact their traffic?
Yeah, for sure.
Augment is a really good one.
If you look at the AI search side, when they started with us, they essentially had zero visibility.
And now they are about 30% to 35% ahead of Cursor, Windsurf, right below GitHub.
They've also seen their citations increase a ton since they weren't getting any beforehand.
And if we look at just traffic, you know, they were around $20,000 monthly organic at the beginning of the partnership.
And also we're spending quite a bit on paid.
Now, they've basically tapered off their paid spending and are about 3x the monthly organic there.
So, Tyler, Windsurf is our product.
Is there, like, potential conflict of interest here if you're working with Augment Code?
Yeah, we, I mean, we work with, like, Brex and Ramp, who compete pretty closely.
We definitely have other examples of competitors.
We've got this policy where we just completely separate, one, our workflows are never, like, being trained on client data or sharing information or inputs between the two workspaces.
Two, we just completely isolate who's working on which account.
And so, you know, it is something that, like, your team, right, needs to ultimately decide, but it's not uncommon to how we've engaged before.
Got it.
Okay, let's keep going.
Could you go back to, actually, that slide of just all the types of...
Yeah.
I really like that.
I'm curious if we can, I don't know, Anna, when I see this, I'm like, okay, comparisons.
I know we have some comparison pages.
Yeah.
That's an interesting thing.
Yeah.
They can go down.
Guides is really interesting.
Like you were saying, like, okay, .NET to Java.
Yeah, I think we need to create a billion of those, like, use case guides, basically.
Like, if we can get those ranking, like, every time someone searches COBOL migration or something, or .NET to Java, and a guide pops up.
Yeah.
That would be really valuable.
Every one of those pages, there's a, like, yeah, use.
Use Devin.
Yeah, just use Devin.
Just ask Devin to do this.
Yeah.
How do you guys approach, like, the research process for the content?
I think that's, like, the critical thing for it to actually be good.
Yeah, 100%.
It's a really important question.
So, we split the engagement into two.
The first one, it's about hitting the quality bar with the content, developing the roadmap that makes sense, right?
That we think is going to drive results over the next 12 months in phase two.
And it's also actually publishing five to 10 articles so that we can see some early signals.
And so what we're doing here is we're basically doing the context engineering by creating what we call artifacts.

And those artifacts are created by ingesting resources that your team sends us around your positioning, your personas, your brands, and also the nuance that we capture from you in like a kickoff call and a product deep dive where we really can hear like in your words, okay, well, why do people choose you versus competitors?
And what makes, you know, your products differentiated?
And what are the nuances about your audience, right, that we might not be able to capture on our own?
And then the other piece is that we have workflows, as I'm sure you can imagine, that are also.
Helping us generate like V1s of these artifacts.
And so really simple example here, right?
With Engine, this is the business travel space type of client, right?
It's like easy to understand.
What we did first is we went and did all the keyword analysis.
We connected it to competitor content.
We started to concentrate the roadmap into different topic clusters and then generate assignments.
For those topics.
And then we also were diving deep into their personas.
For them, industry-specific content was really important.
industries.
And then what that turns into on our side are these artifacts that are the context for every piece of content that we're creating.
And so if you look at the engine example, we made it industry-specific and we go to SuperD, right?
Like, what is the engine secret sauce?
And then what is everything connected?
Connected to the audience that they're going after and what might come up in a chat GPT or, you know, cloud or whatever type of search.

Like, did you talk to people on the engine team?
Did you look at pieces of, like, collateral from their product marketing team?
Like, very tactically, how did you create a document like this?

Yeah, it would be from the resources that they share with us, the conversations that we have with them, and then the workflows that we have that help us do more deep research to actually generate V1s of these artifacts.
And then over time, right, we're reviewing these with you, we're evolving them.
This is something that came from, right, weeks of working together, not just like a one-off kind of one-shot.
This is, like, what, this is, like, the most important artifact from, like, those first eight weeks, you're saying?
Sorry, um, what was the question on that?
What's the most important artifact?
So during those first eight weeks, this is one of the most important artifacts that you're creating.
I guess all of these are the artifacts that the first eight weeks are about.
It's all research and whatever.
And then that allows in the second phase to context everything.
I'm kind of just saying stuff out loud.
I get it.
I heard what you're saying.
And yeah, I would say the most important artifacts and what we generate like week two.
So we generate these really fast.
It's not like we need like a bunch of conversations to create them.
We've got workflows that help us put these together.
So we could technically like the Fathom to you once just use the website and the workflows that we've used your website and the workflows that we've built to generate like a V1 of these artifacts.
This company context is the personas.
And then it's the writing guidelines because that informs who's it written for, who's writing it.
And how should it be written?
I think it would be really important to talk to people on the team.
I think there's just a lot living inside people's heads that would be important to get out of their heads.
We have GTM engineers that are really, really close on a lot of these use cases, for example.
So I think talking to them will be really important.
I think the other follow-up question I have is a lot of our product content is going to be quite technical.
How do you guys think about maintaining the quality bar and ensuring the technical detail is correct?
Like if it's a guide about .NET to Java or something.
Yeah, for sure.
I think in scenarios like that, it would definitely help to get any documentation that exists.
We would want to leverage that as inputs for the workflows.
And then just making sure that we're not expecting like day one to be writing something that only your engineers really had the expertise around.
We would want to maybe look at.
Since there's so much greenfield here, just the initial topics, right?
That can start to develop some signals, start to drive some initial traffic or visibility.

And then as we are getting more confident around understanding the product, having the right docs, building that into the workflows, we would just want a tight review cycle with the technical folks from Cognition that would review, right?
One of the guides that we may put together that is a more specific, right?
Like how to do something with one tool that's connected to your product.
Does that make sense?
It's like an evolution, right?
It's not like an overnight for something like that.
Is the augment code like a good example of that?
I guess I'm just, I feel like this will end up being really, really important.
And it's also like a big part of our company culture, like putting out stuff that's like very technically precise.
And like most of the people that write public-facing technical content are like pretty technical themselves.
What's your take on this?
For sure.
I would look.
Look at some of the Augment Code articles, right?
Because they're technical, but a lot of them were not like, how to migrate from this platform or how these platforms integrate.
And then it might be helpful to bring in Jason from our team who actually worked on the Webflow account and created these integration pages.
I mean, these are very detailed, right?
Like, how to integrate Stripe with Webflow, what you can build, frequently asked questions.
And so I think sort of going through the how of a page like this would probably be directly relevant to the use case that you have in mind.
Got it.
How do you think about, like, quantity versus quality?

Like, I imagine there's sort of, like, you can produce these pieces of content in a super in-depth way where you're, like, talking to a bunch of people on the team and, like, making sure it's, like, the nth degree of good.
Or you can generate a lot of, or you can...
Systematize it more, like the simplest version of that is just use what's publicly available and generate a bunch of content.
How do you think about the right equation to drive business impact?
That's a great question.
I feel like the answer is going to be different for every single client, right?
I think the first thing is just making sure you know which prompts that you want to track and where you want to show up.

And then if we can really tackle, right, those questions from a variety of angles, augment code is honestly a great example because they care a lot about, they have this good context engine for understanding large code bases.
And so you can see a lot of these articles, they're somewhat similar, right?
Context engines versus traditional code search, best AI coding tools, why some AI coding tools break.
What else do we have here?
Prompt context analysis.

But the idea is, you know, if we can create really high quality content that is addressing some gaps in the market, or that gives better answers than existing content that's out there, then we can start to position, you know, your brand in a way that is appearing in more answers.

And that is going to, you know, maybe it's not as helpful as someone who's looking up a specific use case that your product supports extremely well, but, you know, they're going to help each other out, right?
And we could probably do less, we'd probably do less initially on, because it would require more review on like a how to do XYZ migration, or how to connect XYZ tools.
But we could do a lot, which would...
You know, give you more shots on goal for more educational content, more comparing different tools in the market, recommending, you different options for doing different things with AI coding tools.

I don't know if that helps or if that is just a bit rambly, but, you I think there's like a balance, right, where they're both going to move the needle in different ways and we can't necessarily do a ton of scale with content that's very product specific until we've really calibrated on how to make those initial articles as accurate as possible.
Okay.
All right.
Should we talk about like how the, I guess, deployment model looks in a bit more depth?
Is that, is there anything else you want to chat about, Hank?
No, let's keep going.
Okay.
Or we can go back to your presentation.
Oh, no, we're good.
Y'all have a couple extra minutes.
I know we're like two minutes away.
Yeah, think we probably can't stay on for too much longer, but I think just if you can go back to the slide with how you structure the engagements, that would be awesome.
Yeah, you bet.
Eight weeks and getting started, and then once we're grooving, what does that look like?
So once we're grooving, that looks like five articles per week, and these articles can be more of the product articles that we've been talking about.
They can be more of the just editorial articles.
We can kind of switch gears as we're going, but the only bottleneck of five is like how fast we get through the review process.
If we send you five drafts at the beginning of the week, can we get those approved in time to stage and publish by the end of the week?
So that's the model between phase one and phase two.
And I think, you know, naturally we...
We would need to show you more about the workflows and how it's different than setting up your custom GPT or something like that.
But that might be a follow-up call, and maybe we can focus it on, like, the Webflow integrations pages, talking about how the workflows were used to create these outputs.
I have a question about your workflow tools.
I mean, are we interfacing with those workflows?
Are you doing that for us?
What kind of team are we working with on your side?
And, like, what are they doing, and what are we doing?
Yeah, for sure.
Right now, everything's done as a white glove service.
It's our internal team that uses this platform.
We will show you what's going on, but you don't, like, log in to the platform.
Over the next, like, three to six months, there will be a connection to the product that you're seeing here, the visibility product that we're launching over the next one to two weeks.
But right now...

It's, you know, more of just a white glove service and your team is reviewing our drafts and giving input on, you know, we may have a huge backlog of articles that we could execute and we'll give you guys recommendations, right?
But you could help point us in the right direction on, okay, we want to focus on these five, right?
Or these topic clusters.
Those are the, the idea right now is we free up as much time on your team by just having you review articles and review artifacts.
Okay.
Okay.
Those are all my questions for now.
Do you have any of yours?
I don't think so.
No.
Tyler, good to meet you.
We haven't met before, right?
No, I don't think so.
I don't think so.
You look familiar, but maybe I've just seen all of the workshops.
Cool.
All right.
Well, we'll be in touch.
Yeah, sounds good.
Thanks, Tyler.
Yeah, no problem.
Do you all want to do a follow-up with Jason to look at the workflows we use to create the integrations pages, or is it pretty exploratory right now in terms of evaluation?
I think we'll follow up within the next few days.
think we need to kind of regroup as a team and think about how we want to proceed.
Yeah.
I assume yes, so I'd love to see that.
just don't know which day.
I'll have Abigail.
It was Abigail who reached out to you before, right, about calendaring.
She's my EA.
We'll be in touch.
We'll set something up for next week.
Yeah, no problem.
Great to meet you.
I'll shoot a follow-up with some of these overview resources, but I appreciate the time and hope to talk soon.
Cool.
Sounds good.
Thanks, Tyler.
Have a good day.
Bye.
See ya.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Evaluate GrowthX's content engine for Cognition's SEO and traffic growth.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=182.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Problem:** Cognition's organic presence lags competitors (Cursor, Anthropic) due to a lean marketing team and "random acts of content."](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=253.0)
  - [**Solution:** GrowthX proposes a white-glove service to build a high-volume content engine, publishing 5 articles/week to rapidly increase domain authority and traffic.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1575.0)
  - [**Critical Concern:** Cognition requires absolute technical precision. GrowthX's proposed solution is a phased approach, starting with high-volume educational content and evolving to complex guides after calibrating with Cognition's technical team.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1218.0)
  - [**Precedent:** GrowthX's work for AugmentCode—a direct competitor to Cognition's Windsurf product—resulted in a 3x increase in organic traffic and 30-35% higher AI search visibility.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=793.0)

## Topics

### Cognition's Content Challenge

  - [**Goal:** Increase organic presence to compete with Cursor and Anthropic for mindshare.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=253.0)
  - [**Constraint:** A lean marketing team; content creation is currently a mix of engineering and GTM teams, lacking a systematic workflow.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=367.0)
  - [**Need:** A "machine" to scale content production and capture the deep technical knowledge currently siloed within the team.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=367.0)

### GrowthX's Proposed Solution

  - [**Model:** A white-glove service using an internal platform (not a self-serve tool) to execute the entire content lifecycle.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1639.0)
  - [**Process:**](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=481.0)
    1.  [**Strategy:** Identify topic clusters for authority.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=503.0)
    2.  [**Execution:** Create and publish content.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=503.0)
    3.  [**Monitoring:** Track performance via the upcoming "Check That" product.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=481.0)
  - [**Engagement Structure:**](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1562.0)
      - [**Phase 1 (8 weeks):** Research, context engineering, and publishing 5–10 articles to establish a baseline.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=956.0)
      - [**Phase 2 (Ongoing):** Publish 5 articles/week, limited only by Cognition's review speed.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1575.0)

### Technical Quality & Precision

  - [**Requirement:** Cognition's culture demands absolute technical precision in all public-facing content.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1218.0)
  - [**GrowthX's Phased Approach:**](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1218.0)
      - [**Initial Focus:** High-volume educational content (e.g., tool comparisons) to build domain authority quickly.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1364.0)
      - [**Evolution:** Progress to complex technical guides (e.g., `.NET to Java` migration) after calibrating workflows with Cognition's technical team.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1218.0)
  - [**Key Input:** Interviews with Cognition's GTM engineers are essential to capture deep, unwritten product knowledge.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1203.0)

### Case Study: AugmentCode

  - [**Context:** AugmentCode is a direct competitor to Cognition's Windsurf product.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=849.0)
  - [**Results:**](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=793.0)
      - [**AI Search Visibility:** 30–35% ahead of Cursor and Windsurf.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=793.0)
      - [**Organic Traffic:** Grew from \~20k/month to \~60k/month (3x increase), enabling a reduction in paid spend.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=793.0)
  - [**Conflict of Interest Policy:** GrowthX isolates client data and teams to prevent information sharing between competitors.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=849.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Anna/Hank:** Regroup internally to evaluate the proposal.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1741.0)
  - [**Anna:** Schedule a follow-up call via EA (Abigail) for next week.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1741.0)
      - [**Purpose:** Review GrowthX's workflows for creating complex technical content, using the Webflow integration pages as an example.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1620.0)
  - [**Tyler:** Send follow-up resources to Anna and Hank.](https://fathom.video/share/xsfs2DEF2VmKdVMxN34v69UoJwFN_fd8?tab=summary&timestamp=1780.0)

action_items.description: Email Anna/Hank: overview resources + Augment Code examples
Schedule follow-up w/ Tyler + Jason re: integrations workflows; include Abigail
